title: 2 Chronicles 27
subtitle: Jotham — Faithful but Brief

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Fall of Northern Kingdom

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — Jotham Does Right; Builds and Conquers

refs.note: Kings gives Jotham four verses with a positive assessment but notes the high places remained. Chronicles adds construction projects and military victory over the Ammonites, showing faithfulness producing tangible results.
Micah prophesied during Jotham's reign, suggesting that despite the king's personal faithfulness, social injustice was already festering beneath the surface of Judahite prosperity.

notes.note: The principle here applies universally: God’s governance is consistent and his responses are proportional to human faithfulness. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Chronicler’s distinctive theological vocabulary shapes the presentation of these events. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Jotham does what is right — “as his father Uzziah had done” but crucially, “he did not enter the temple.” He learned from his father’s mistake. He builds the Upper Gate of the temple, fortifies Ophel, defeats the Ammonites, and receives massive tribute. Nine verses for a good king. The Chronicler is brief because Jotham’s story has no dramatic crisis.

heb.paragraph: Bayit (house) carries a deliberate double meaning in Chronicles: Solomon builds God a house (temple), and God promises David a house (dynasty). The interplay between these two 'houses' generates the theological tension that drives the entire narrative: will the dynasty endure? Will the temple remain? Both depend on covenant faithfulness.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 5‐9 — Summary: “He Grew Powerful Because He Walked Steadfastly”

refs.note: Kings mentions that during Jotham's reign the LORD began to send Rezin and Pekah against Judah — the Syro-Ephraimite threat that would explode under Ahaz. Chronicles omits this, keeping Jotham's record entirely positive.
The summary 'he grew powerful because he walked steadfastly before the LORD' echoes his father Uzziah's early reign. Unlike Uzziah, Jotham maintained that steadfastness to the end.

notes.note: Calvin: On 9 — Summary: “He Grew Powerful Because He Walked Steadfastly”: teaches that all human religious activity is ultimately provisional — when God himself appears, our best efforts are suspended in awe. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 9 — Summary: “He Grew Powerful Because He Walked Steadfastly”: Gen 22:2. Jewish tradition universally accepted this identification, which became foundational for Jerusalem’s theology of sacred space. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: “Jotham grew powerful because he walked steadfastly before the LORD his God.” The Chronicler’s thesis in one sentence: steady faithfulness produces power. The brevity of the account is itself a commentary — a faithful, undramatic reign doesn’t need many words. He dies and is buried in the City of David. “Ahaz his son succeeded him.”

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

ppl.name: Ahaz
ppl.text: Ahaz was Jotham's son who succeeded him as king, but is not a key figure in Jotham's own reign described in 2 Chronicles 27.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVJotham waged war against the king of the Ammonites and conquered them. That year the Ammonites paid him a hundred talents of silver, ten thousand cors of wheat and ten thousand cors of barley. The Ammonites brought him the same amount also in the second and third years.ESVHe fought with the king of the Ammonites and prevailed against them. And the Ammonites gave him that year 100 talents of silver, and 10,000 cors of wheat and 10,000 of barley. The Ammonites paid him the same amount in the second and the third years.NIVJotham rebuilt the Upper Gate of the temple of the Lord and did extensive work on the wall at the hill of Ophel.ESVHe built the upper gate of the house of the LORD and did much building on the wall of Ophel.

coaching.tip: Jotham 'grew powerful because he walked steadfastly before the LORD his God.' The chapter is brief — a quiet, faithful reign without dramatic events. The Chronicler shows that not every good king needs a crisis to prove his faith. Consistency is its own story.

prayer_prompt: Jotham did right but briefly; the people still acted corruptly. Consider asking God about faithful leadership in corrupt contexts — what's possible and what isn't.
